_edit_guide.change an amount: Edit that holding's 'alloc'. Then DELETE its 'units','cost','cost_price' so they re-derive. Re-run.
_edit_guide.add a stock: Copy a stock block. Set name, ticker (Yahoo symbol: NSE=.NS, BSE=.BO), alloc, sleeve, sector, benchmark. Re-run.
_edit_guide.add a fund: Copy a fund block. Set name, query (full fund name incl. 'Direct Growth'), alloc, sleeve, benchmark. Re-run.
_edit_guide.benchmark value: One tag e.g. 'nifty50tri', or a weighted blend e.g. {'nifty50tri':0.6,'debtidx':0.4}. Tags must exist in benchmark_proxies.
_edit_guide.note: Allocs need NOT sum to a round number — invested capital = sum of allocs, computed automatically. This _edit_guide is ignored by the engine.

thesis.headline: A growth-tilted, all-weather Indian book — built to beat its own allocation, not a convenient index.
body: Objective. Compound a ₹10L base at a premium to a passive portfolio of the *same* asset-allocation. The bar is deliberately honest: not Nifty 50, but a blended benchmark weighted to exactly how the money is actually deployed.

Construction. ~85% equity / ~15% debt. A large-cap core (direct blue chips + a bluechip fund) for stability, a flexi/multi-cap engine (Parag Parikh, HDFC Flexi, contra/value) for the alpha sleeve, and a deliberate mid/small-cap tilt (Dixon, Kotak Emerging, Nippon Small) for the compounding tail. Direct-equity names lean into coverage I know — industrials, defence and electronics manufacturing (L&T, BEL, Dixon).

Why a blended benchmark. Benchmarking a multi-cap, part-debt book to the Nifty 50 is the oldest trick for manufacturing alpha that isn't there. Each sleeve is marked to its right index — large-cap to Nifty 50 TRI, flexi to Nifty 500 TRI, mid to Midcap 150 TRI, small to Smallcap 250 TRI, debt to a gilt index — then re-aggregated at the portfolio's own weights. Beating this means the *selection* worked, not the asset mix.

Total-return, always. Every index leg is a Total Return (TRI) proxy — tracked through a low-cost index fund's NAV, which captures reinvested dividends net of ~15–20 bps. Comparing an equity book against a price index (ex-dividend) is a ~1.5% p.a. free kick I'm not interested in claiming.

What 'live' means here. Direct equities mark intraday off NSE; mutual funds have one NAV a day, so that sleeve marks to the latest declared NAV. Risk and attribution stats are computed on the full daily curve reconstructed from each holding's price/NAV history since inception.
